author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Preface to the Introductions to Astrology
text: PREFACE The present volume offers a critical edition of the Hebrew texts, accompa- nied by English translation and commentary, of Reshit Hokhmah (Beginning of Wisdom) and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot (Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs), by Abraham Ibn Ezra (ca. 1089-ca. 1161). The first, the summa and by far the longest of his astrological works, the target of the most cross-references from the rest of that corpus, and the most influential, enjoyed the widest cir- culation among Jews in the Middle Ages and after. The second, by contrast, is the most obscure. It is never referred to elsewhere by its author and is the only for which Ibn Ezra’s authorship must be substantiated. I have placed them together in the present volume because the allocation of works to the several volumes of this series was done thematically, by astrological genre; and Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot are Ibn Ezra’s two intro- ductions to astrology. As such, the two were written in order to explain concepts common to the various branches of astrology that Ibn Ezra addressed elsewhere and to elucidate the worldview that underlies astrology. Consequently, these two treatises are the richest and most varied with regard to the astrological information they present. By the same token, their content is the most complex of all of his astrological writings. Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot also exemplify the close collaboration between astronomy and astrology in medieval science and are the two components of Ibn Ezra’s astrological corpus with the most extensive, comprehensive, and significant astronomical content. This volume casts new light on our knowledge of Ibn Ezra’s sources. My study of Reshit Hokhmah discovered that at least one-fourth of its text consists of verbatim translations or close paraphrases of known Arabic astrological texts. Their collation with the Hebrew text of Reshit Hokhmah demonstrates that Abraham Ibn Ezra was a translator of astrological texts from Arabic to Hebrew and allows us to start to understand his methods of translation. This volume completes the publication of Ibn Ezra’s extant Hebrew astrological writings. This does not conclude the series, however, because a number of his treatises are extant only in Latin translation, the Hebrew original not surviving, or may have actually been written in Latin, with Ibn Ezra’s active participation. They too deserve to be studied and edited. x PREFACE Readers may well ask why Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot were deferred to the last volume of Ibn Ezra’s extant Hebrew astrologi- cal writings. Given that they are introduction to astrology, one might have thought they should be the first volume of this series. A number of consid- erations dictated the order followed. To start with, Reshit Hokhmah is the only astrological text by Ibn Ezra that had appeared in a critical edition plus English translation before I began this series. Although I always thought it essential to improve that edition and translation, accompanied by a commentary to help modern readers understand the obscure astrological concepts immersed in it, I realized that the task was less urgent and could be postponed to a later time. Second, one of the main objectives of the commentaries that accompany the critical editions and translations of this series is to display the links among the works included in each volume and between them and other parts of Ibn Ezra’s astrological corpus. Because Reshit Hokhmah and Mish- petei ha-Mazzalot address concepts related to all the branches of astrol- ogy and are accordingly closely related to all his other astrological works, I thought it better to wait with them until I had finished with all the other astrological treatises, so that readers could have access to all the loci that can illustrate these links. Lastly, I realized from the outset that Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot pose a formidable challenge to the scholar, because they are the richest, most varied, and most complex of all Ibn Ezra’s astrological treatises. Aware that I would need to be familiar with all the minutiae of the specialized branches of astrology in order to solve the numerous difficulties posed by these two treatises, I decided to make them the last Hebrew component of the series. I wish to express my sincere gratitude to a number of people who have contributed toward the realization of this volume. Keiji Yamamoto and Charles Burnett placed at my disposal their edition and translation of the Arabic text of Abii Ma‘shar’s Great Introduction to Astrology before its pub- lication. Lenn Schramm revised the translations and the English sections of this book; he also made very helpful suggestions about the interpretation of the Hebrew texts. The Israel Science Foundation (Grant No. 17/12) pro- vided a generous grant. This book benefited from my fellowship at the Paris Institute for Advanced Studies (France), with the financial support of the French Republic, managed by the Agence Nationale de la Recherche, pro- gramme “Investissements d’ avenir” (ANR-11]-LABX-0027-01 Labex RFIEA+). My warmest thanks to all of them. Sh. S. June 2016
translation: This volume presents a critical edition of the Hebrew texts, with English translation and commentary, of Reshit Hokhmah (Beginning of Wisdom) and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot (Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs), by Abraham Ibn Ezra (c. 1089-c. 1161). Reshit Hokhmah, the most extensive and influential of his astrological works, was widely circulated among Jews in the Middle Ages. Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, in contrast, is more obscure and its authorship by Ibn Ezra requires substantiation. I have grouped them in this volume because they are both introductions to astrology, explaining fundamental concepts and the underlying worldview. These treatises are rich in astrological information and complex in content, demonstrating the close collaboration between astronomy and astrology in medieval science. This volume sheds new light on Ibn Ezra's sources, revealing that a significant portion of Reshit Hokhmah consists of translations or paraphrases of Arabic astrological texts, indicating Ibn Ezra's role as a translator. This publication completes Ibn Ezra's extant Hebrew astrological writings, though some treatises exist only in Latin translation or may have been originally written in Latin. Readers may wonder why these introductory works were placed in the final volume. Reshit Hokhmah had a prior critical edition, making its immediate re-editing less urgent. Furthermore, these works connect to all branches of astrology, so it was deemed best to publish them after other specialized treatises were available. Lastly, their complexity made them a formidable challenge, requiring familiarity with all astrological minutiae, hence their placement as the final Hebrew component. I extend my gratitude to Keiji Yamamoto and Charles Burnett for their edition of Abū Maʿshar’s Great Introduction to Astrology, Lenn Schramm for his revisions and suggestions, the Israel Science Foundation for its grant, and the Paris Institute for Advanced Studies for its financial support. Sh. S. June 2016

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Abraham Ibn Ezra's Life and Works
text: אברהם אבן עזרא (בערך 1089-בערך 1161) בילה את חמשת העשורים הראשונים לחייו בספרד המוסלמית, שם קיבל את השכלתו היהודית והמדעית במסגרת התרבות והשפה הערבית. בשלב מוקדם זה הוא כנראה התפרנס כמשורר, נדד מפטרון אחד למשנהו, ולא כתב כמעט דבר הקשור לפרשנות מקראית ומדע, שני התחומים שבהם הצטיין בחלק השני של הקריירה שלו! הוא עזב את מולדתו בגיל 50 והחל חיים נודדים שלקחו אותו דרך איטליה, צרפת ואנגליה. בשנים אלו כתב רבות על מגוון רחב של נושאים. פרסומו של אברהם אבן עזרא נובע מפירושיו המקראיים המצוינים, אך הוא כתב גם שירה דתית וחילונית וסדרה של מונוגרפיות דתיות-תיאולוגיות וחיבורים דקדוקיים. תחומי העניין האינטלקטואליים שלו התרחבו גם למדעים. גוף יצירותיו המדעי, הכולל יותר משלושים חיבורים, עוסק במתמטיקה, אסטרונומיה, מכשירים וכלים מדעיים, ובלוח השנה היהודי; אך במיוחד באסטרולוגיה. תכונה מהותית של גוף יצירות עצום ומגוון זה היא שהוא נכתב כמעט אך ורק בעברית. אין זו הגזמה לומר שאילולא הגירתו הכפויה של אבן עזרא מספרד המוסלמית לאירופה הלטינית, שלוויתה במעבר מערבית לעברית ובהרחבת פעילותו הספרותית לתחומי פרשנות המקרא והמדע, הוא היה נשכח כחוקר.
translation: Abraham Ibn Ezra (circa 1089-circa 1161) spent the first five decades of his life in Muslim Spain, where he received his Jewish and scientific education within the orbit of Arabic culture and language. In this early stage, he apparently made his living as a poet, traveling from one patron to another, and wrote almost nothing related to biblical exegesis and science, the two fields in which he was to excel in the second part of his career! He left his homeland at the age of 50 and began an itinerant life that took him through Italy, France, and England. During these years, he wrote prolifically on a wide variety of subjects. Abraham Ibn Ezra's fame stems from his excellent biblical commentaries, but he also wrote religious and secular poetry and a series of religious-theological monographs and grammatical treatises. His intellectual interests also extended to the sciences. His scientific corpus, comprising more than thirty treatises, deals with mathematics, astronomy, scientific instruments and tools, and the Jewish calendar; but especially with astrology. An essential feature of this huge and multifarious corpus is that it was written almost exclusively in Hebrew. It is no overstatement to say that were it not for Ibn Ezra's forced emigration from Muslim Spain to Latin Europe, which was accompanied by a transition from Arabic to Hebrew and by the broadening of his literary activities to the fields of biblical exegesis and science, he would have fallen into oblivion as a scholar.
chunk_ref: ), Isagoge in Astrologiam, chapter 26, sig. E2r. idem, Liber primus, de gentibus

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Ibn Ezra's Astrological Influence
text: מימי הביניים ועד היום, התפתחות האסטרולוגיה בקרב יהודים נקשרה בשמו של אברהם אבן עזרא. עבור התרבות הלטינית של ימי הביניים, אברהם אבנזרא היה בעיקר מתווך ומשדר של מדע ואסטרולוגיה ערבית לאירופה של המאה השתים עשרה. אף על פי שחיבוריו האסטרולוגיים של אבן עזרא נכתבו בעברית ובוודאי כוונו לקוראים עבריים, זמן קצר לאחר מותו הם החלו להיות מועברים לקוראים לא-יהודים, כאשר אוספים של חיבוריו האסטרולוגיים של אבן עזרא תורגמו בגלים חוזרים ונשנים ללטינית ולשפות העממיות האירופיות המתפתחות. מנקודת מבט יהודית, תרומתו של אברהם אבן עזרא הייתה שונה וחשובה בשתי דרכים. ראשית, על ידי שילוב רעיונות אסטרולוגיים בפרשנותו המקראית המשפיעה, הוא קידם את הקליטה החלקה של תוכן אסטרולוגי לליבת התרבות היהודית. בנוסף, הוא יצר את הגוף המקיף הראשון של ספרי לימוד אסטרולוגיים בעברית המטפלים במערכות העיקריות של האסטרולוגיה הערבית וסיפק לקוראים עבריים גישה רחבה לאסטרולוגיה. אם לשפוט לפי מספר העותקים הקיימים בכתבי יד, חיבוריו האסטרולוגיים העבריים של אבן עזרא הופצו באופן נרחב בקרב יהודים. הישגו האסטרולוגי מעולם לא חזר על עצמו, לא בימי הביניים ולא בעת החדשה. הודות לתגליות אחרונות, כיום אנו יודעים על תשעה עשר חיבורים שלו, העוסקים בז'אנרים העיקריים של הספרות האסטרולוגית היוונית והערבית, כדלקמן:
translation: From the Middle Ages until the present, the development of astrology among Jews has been associated with the name of Abraham Ibn Ezra. For medieval Latin culture, Abraham Avenezra was mainly an intermediary and transmitter of Arabic science and astrology to twelfth-century Europe. Although Ibn Ezra's astrological treatises were written in Hebrew and certainly targeted Hebrew readers, shortly after his death they began to be transmitted to non-Jewish readers, as collections of Ibn Ezra's astrological treatises were translated in repeated waves into Latin and the emerging European vernaculars. From a Jewish perspective, Abraham Ibn Ezra's contribution was different and important in two ways. First, by incorporating astrological ideas into his influential biblical exegesis, he promoted the smooth absorption of astrological content into the hard core of Jewish culture. In addition, he created the first comprehensive corpus of Hebrew astrological textbooks that address the main systems of Arabic astrology and provided Hebrew readers with wide access to astrology. Judging by the numbers of extant manuscript copies, Ibn Ezra's Hebrew astrological treatises circulated widely among Jews. His astrological achievement was never repeated, neither in the Middle Ages nor in the Modern Era. Thanks to recent discoveries, today we know of nineteen treatises by him, addressing the main genres of Greek and Arabic astrological literature, as follows:

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Introductions to Astrology
text: מבואות לאסטרולוגיה, המעבירים יסודות בסיסיים של תפיסת העולם העומדת בבסיס האסטרולוגיה ומסבירים מושגים טכניים של הענפים השונים של האסטרולוגיה: (1-3) הגרסה השלמה הקיימת של ספר ראשית חכמה וספר משפטי המזלות; שני חיבורים אלו נערכו בכרך זה. הגרסה השנייה שהתגלתה לאחרונה של ראשית חכמה (להלן ראשית חכמה ב') פורסמה לאחרונה במחקר נפרד.
translation: Introductions to astrology, conveying basic elements of the worldview underlying astrology and explaining technical concepts of the various branches of astrology: (1-3) The extant complete version of Sefer Reshit Hokhmah (Book of the Beginning of Wisdom) and Sefer Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot (Book of the Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs); these two works are edited in the present volume. The recently discovered second version of Reshit Hokhmah (henceforth Reshit Hokhmah II) was recently published in a separate study.
chunk_ref: ), Isagoge in Astrologiam, chapter 26, sig. E2r. idem, Liber primus, de gentibus

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Treatises on Astrological Reasons
text: חיבורים המסבירים את הסיבות האסטרולוגיות מאחורי המושגים המועסקים בשתי הגרסאות של ראשית חכמה: (4-5) שתי הגרסאות של ספר הטעמים (להלן טעמים א' וטעמים ב'). המהדורות הביקורתיות של הטקסטים העבריים של שני פירושים אלו, מלוות בתרגום לאנגלית ובפירוש, פורסמו בכרך הראשון של סדרה זו.
translation: Treatises explaining the astrological reasons behind the concepts employed in both versions of Reshit Hokhmah: (4-5) The two versions of Sefer ha-Te'amim (Book of Reasons; henceforth Teamim I and Teamim II). The critical editions of the Hebrew texts of these two commentaries, accompanied by an English translation and commentary, were published in the first volume of this series.
chunk_ref: ), Isagoge in Astrologiam, chapter 26, sig. E2r. idem, Liber primus, de gentibus

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Nativities and Destiny
text: לידות, הקובעות כי גורל היילוד נקבע על ידי תצורת הגופים השמימיים ברגע הלידה וניתן ללמוד אותו מתוך מפת הלידה ההורוסקופית: (6-8) הגרסה השלמה הקיימת של ספר המולדות (להלן מולדות). המהדורה הביקורתית של הטקסט העברי של מולדות, מלווה בתרגום לאנגלית ובפירוש, פורסמה בכרך הרביעי של סדרה זו. הגרסה השנייה של ספר המולדות שרדה כיום בתרגום לטיני בשם Liber nativitatum (להלן Nativitatum). חיבור לטיני בשם Liber de nativitatibus (להלן Nativitatibus) הוא כנראה תרגום של טקסט עברי אבוד של אבן עזרא, אם כי לא ניתן לשלול שהוא חובר בהשתתפותו הפעילה של אבן עזרא. מהדורה ביקורתית של שני טקסטים לטיניים אלו, מלווה בתרגומים לאנגלית ובפירושים, מתוכננת לכרך הבא של סדרה זו.
translation: Nativities, which posit that the destiny of the newborn is determined by the configuration of the celestial bodies at the instant of birth and may be learned from the natal horoscopic chart: (6-8) The complete extant version of Sefer ha-Moladot (Book of Nativities; henceforth Moladot). The critical edition of the Hebrew text of Moladot, accompanied by an English translation and commentary, was published in volume four of this series. The second version of Sefer ha-Moladot survives today in a Latin translation entitled Liber nativitatum (henceforth Nativitatum). A Latin treatise entitled Liber de nativitatibus (henceforth Nativitatibus) is probably a translation of a lost Hebrew text by Ibn Ezra, although it cannot be ruled out that it was composed with Ibn Ezra's active participation. A critical edition of these two Latin texts, accompanied by English translations and commentaries, is planned for the next volume of this series.
chunk_ref: ), Isagoge in Astrologiam, chapter 26, sig. E2r. idem, Liber primus, de gentibus

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Zodiacal Signs and Planetary Dignities
text: המזלות וחלקי הכוכבים, התלויים במקום הכוכבים במזלות. בעוד שרשית חכמה מקצה כמעט את כל פרק 2 לנושא זה – כמעט מחצית מהחיבור כולו – משפטי המזלות מקדיש לו מספר קטעים רצופים לאחר ההקדמה. ראה להלן לפרטים על תוכני חלק זה של רשית חכמה ומשפטי המזלות.
translation: The zodiacal signs and the planetary dignities, which depend on the position of the planets in the signs. While Reshit Hokhmah dedicates almost all of chapter 2 to this subject – nearly half of the entire treatise – Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot devotes several continuous sections to it after the introduction. See below for details on the contents of this part of Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot.
chunk_ref: Sela 2013, part II (“Rectification of the Nativity”), section 4, sentence 4, on

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Aspects, Quadrants, and Horoscopic Places
text: ההיבטים, רביעי ההורוסקופ, ושנים עשר המקומות ההורוסקופיים וסימניהם. נושאים אלה תופסים את כל פרק 3 ברשית חכמה; משפטי המזלות מתייחס אליהם במספר קטעים לא רציפים ונוקט גישה שונה כלפיהם.
translation: The aspects, the quadrants of the horoscopic chart, and the twelve horoscopic places and their indications. These topics occupy all of chapter 3 in Reshit Hokhmah; Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot addresses them in a number of discontinuous sections and takes a different approach to them.
chunk_ref: Sela 2013, part II (“Rectification of the Nativity”), section 4, sentence 4, on

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Natures and Significances of Seven Planets
text: טבעי הכוכבים השבעה ומשמעותם. גם רשית חכמה וגם משפטי המזלות מארגנים נושא זה בשבעה קטעים רציפים (כל פרק 4 ברשית חכמה). למרות ששני החיבורים מטפלים בכוכבים בסדר שונה, תיאוריהם דומים (אך לא זהים).
translation: The natures and significances of the seven planets. Both Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot organize this topic into seven continuous sections (all of chapter 4 in Reshit Hokhmah). Although the two works treat the planets in a different order, their accounts of them are similar (though not identical).
chunk_ref: Sela 2013, part II (“Rectification of the Nativity”), section 4, sentence 4, on

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Planets' Fortune, Misfortune, Power, and Weakness
text: מזלם וחסרונם של הכוכבים וכוחם וחולשתם, כתלות בתנועתם במסלוליהם, או בשינוי מיקומם.
translation: The planets' fortune and misfortune, and their power and weakness, as a function of their motion in their orbs, or of their changing position.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Decan Lords of the Signs
text: הראשון קשור לחלק של משפטי המזלות המפרט את שליטי שלושת הדקאנים בכל מזל. מה שתופס את עינו של הקורא הוא שחלק זה, על פי כל כתבי היד שבדקתי, מאורגן לשלושה חלקים לא הגיוניים ולא רציפים: הראשון מתחיל עם שליטי שלושת הדקאנים של סרטן (לא של טלה, כצפוי) ומסתיים עם שליטי הדקאנים של דגים; החלק השלישי (לא השני) מתחיל עם שליטי שלושת הדקאנים של טלה ומסתיים עם שליטי הדקאנים של תאומים; ואילו באמצע הקטע, בין שתי הרשימות הנפרדות, אנו נתקלים בכותרת "שליטי הדקאנים" (שציפינו למצוא בתחילת הקטע), ואחריה הסבר קצר כיצד נקבע שליט הדקאן עבור מפה הורוסקופית ספציפית.
translation: The first relates to the section of Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot that lists the lords of the three decans in each sign. What catches the reader's eye is that this section, according to all the manuscripts I checked, is organized into three illogical and discontinuous parts: the first begins with the lords of the three decans of Cancer (not of Aries, as expected) and ends with the lords of the decans of Pisces; the third part (not the second) begins with the lords of the three decans of Aries and ends with the lords of the decans of Gemini; while in the middle of the section, between the two separate lists, we encounter the rubric "Lords of the decans" (which we would expect to find at the very beginning of the section), followed by a short explanation of how the lord of the decan is determined for some specific horoscopic chart.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Indications of Horoscopic Places
text: הדוגמה השנייה קשורה לאינדיקציות של שנים עשר המקומות ההורוסקופיים, אשר משפטי המזלות, בכל כתבי היד שבדקתי, מארגן לשלושה חלקים לא רציפים ולא הגיוניים, כמו בדוגמה הקודמת. החלק הראשון כולל את האינדיקציות של המקומות ההורוסקופיים הראשון עד השישי. בנקודה זו, התיאור מופרע באופן בלתי צפוי והטקסט פונה לגישתו של חנוך ל"תיקון הלידה", מה שמכונה trutina Hermetis (מאזני הרמס), לפיה מיקום הירח ברגע הלידה הוא האופק ברגע ההתעברות, ולהיפך. גם תיאור זה מחולק לשלושה חלקים לא רציפים: הראשון מתאר את trutina Hermetis ומדווח על חוויותיו של אבן עזרא בעניין זה; החלק השני קוטע את תיאור trutina Hermetis ודן בחלוקת חודשי ההיריון בין כוכבי הלכת, לפי הסדר התלמאי של מסלוליהם; והחלק השלישי חוזר לטיפול ב-trutina Hermetis עם טיפול בהיבטיה היישומיים. בנקודה זו, משפטי המזלות חוזר לתיאור המופרע של המקומות ההורוסקופיים ומציג את האינדיקציות של המקומות ההורוסקופיים השביעי עד השנים עשר.
translation: The second example relates to indications of the twelve horoscopic places, which Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, in all manuscripts I checked, organizes into three discontinuous and illogical parts, as in the previous example. The first part has the indications of the first through the sixth horoscopic places. At this point, the account is unexpectedly interrupted and the text turns to Enoch’s approach to the “rectification of the nativity,” the so-called trutina Hermetis (Hermes’ balance), according to which the position of the Moon at the moment of birth is the ascendant at the moment of conception, and vice versa. This account, too, is divided into three discontinuous parts: the first describes the trutina Hermetis and reports Ibn Ezra’s own experiences in this regard; the second part interrupts the account of the trutina Hermetis and discusses the distribution of the months of gestation among the planets, in the Ptolemaic order of their orbs; and the third part resumes the treatment of the trutina Hermetis with a treatment of its applicative aspects. At this point, Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot resumes the interrupted account of the horoscopic places and presents the indications of the seventh through twelfth horoscopic places.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Human Soul and Divine Deliverance
text: ובעבור כי נשמת האדם גבוהה מן האמצעי, אם היתה הנפש חכמה והבירה מעשה השם, שהםבלא אמצעי ועל ידי אמצעי, והניחה תאות העולם השפל, והתבודדה לדבקה בשם הנכבד--אםיש במערכת הכוכבים בעת ההריון רעה שתבוא עליו ביוסידוע, השם, שדבק בו, יסבב סבות להצילו מרעתו
translation: And because the human soul is higher than the intermediary [world], if the soul is wise and understands the work of God, which is both without intermediary and through an intermediary, and if it abandons the desires of the lower world, and secludes itself to cleave to the Glorious Name—then, if in the configuration of the stars at the time of conception there is evil that would come upon him at a certain time, God, to whom he cleaves, will cause reasons to save him from his evil.

title: Soul's Ascent to Upper Beings
text: תקחני--מלת 'לקיחה' בלא 'דבר' הוא נשמת הצדיק עם העליונים, שאינם גוף ולא ימותו לעולם
translation: 'Took me'—the word 'taking' without 'a thing' means the soul of the righteous person with the uppermost beings, who are not corporeal and will never die.

translation: 'Thou wilt make known to me'—for when the body dies, 'Thou wilt make known to me' the path of 'life'; and the meaning is: the path by which I will ascend to the heavens to be with the uppermost angels.

title: Fixed Stars and Their Astrological Data
text: כל אחד מהכוכבים האלה מלווה ב: (א) השם המתורגם לעברית, ובמקרים בודדים גם השם הערבי שמאחורי השם העברי, מתועתק באותיות עבריות; (ב) קו הרוחב וקו האורך האקליפטי שלו בזמן חיבור רשית חכמה; (ג) גודלו (רובם מהגודל הראשון והשני, אך נכללים גם כוכבים עמומים וערפיליות); ו-(ד) נתונים אסטרולוגיים הקשורים אליו בדרך כלל, כגון תערובת או טבעו הפלנטרי, ובמקרים מסוימים, האם הוא אחד הכוכבים הגורמים למוות.
translation: Each of these stars is accompanied by: (a) its Hebrew translated name, and in a very few cases also the Arabic name behind the Hebrew name, transcribed in Hebrew characters; (b) its ecliptical latitude and longitude at the time of the composition of Reshit Hokhmah; (c) its magnitude (most are of the first and second magnitude, but dim stars and nebulae are also included); and (d) astrological data usually associated with it, such as its planetary mixture or nature, and, in some cases, whether it is one of the stars that cause death.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Zodiacal Orb Precession
text: בי בכ מאה שנה יתגלגל גלגל המזלות ממערב למזרח קרוב ממעלה וחצי. והנה בימי בטלמיוס החכם, ואין לו רק אלף שנים, מצא לב האריה על שתים מעלות, והנה היום על שמונה עשרה.
translation: For the orb of the zodiacal signs revolves from west to east approximately one and a half degrees every century. And behold, in the days of the wise Ptolemy, which was only a thousand years ago, he found Regulus at two degrees, and behold, today it is at eighteen degrees.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Authorship, Date, and Place of Composition
text: Mazzalot. In fact, nowhere in his oeuvre does Ibn Ezra make a cross- reference to a work named Sefer Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot; nor can we be certain that it was the author himself who choose that title for the book, even though it appears as such in all the manuscripts I checked. Since the phrase mishpetei ha-mazzalot appears at least five times in its text, meaning ‘judgments of the signs, it is entirely possible that some copyist in the early stages of the transmission of the original text decided to make that the title of the book. Nevertheless, Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot can be safely ascribed to Ibn Ezra for two reasons. The first is that it includes two explicit cross-references to other works by him. One is to Sefer ha-Moladot (Book of Nativities), which probably means the lost second version of Ibn Ezra’s Sefer ha-Moladot. The other is to Sefer ha-Luhot, which would be one of the versions of Ibn Ezra’s astronomical tables. The second reason is that all of the Hebrew terminology of Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, with one exception, can be found elsewhere in Ibn Ezra’s oeuvre, including some rare terms or usages. Indeed, their very rarity creates a terminological link between Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot and the treatises where they appear, allowing us to narrow the range in place and time for Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot's composition. These links sug- gest that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot was written late in Ibn Ezra’s career, when he was living in Rouen or in England. This possibility is strengthened by the report in Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot of an astronomical observation made with an astrolabe at latitude 50.5°, which is approximately that of Rouen. The first reference is about how the Moon's power, in comparison with the power of the other planets, changes when it is in the lunar mansions. The second reference is about how the power of a planet, when it is in a large horoscopic place, varies in comparison to its power when it is in an “equal place” (1W” n'a) or “place of wind” (nin na), and how the power of a planet, when it is in a short horoscopic place, varies in comparison to its power when it is in a “strained place” (pinT n'a). Neither of these topics is mentioned in Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot and the terminology of the second reference is never used in Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot or any other part of Ibn Ezra’s astrological treatises. To denote the eighth orb, Mm §1:1 uses the expression 91737 53537, “the great orb.” See note there. Asa rule, Ibn Ezra uses this term to denote the eccentric circle. But the same usage occurs in Yesod mora’ (The Foundation of piety), amonograph written by Ibn Ezra in England in 1158. For astrological lots, Mm § 51:1 uses 111/; for fixed signs (Taurus, Leo, Scorpio), Mm § 2:3 uses 0°23. As a rule, Ibn Ezra uses גורלות‎ and עומדים‎ for these concepts. See notes there. But these two rare usages also occur in Tequfah, where the year 4921 AM = 1160/1CE is mentioned in an illustration. See Tequfah, § 16:3, § 18:1, § 19:3, 385-387, and notes. See Mm § 13:2 and note.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Ibn Ezra's Other Introductions to Astrology
text: Other than Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, Ibn Ezra composed or was involved to some extent in the composition of a number of treatises that fall into the genre of the introduction to astrology. These are the two versions of Sefer ha-Te‘amim, the second version of Reshit Hokhmah, and Ysagoge, which is the first part of the Epitome totius astrologiae, a twelfth- century popular Latin work that has been considered to fall within the ambit of Ibn Ezra’s astrological works. Whereas the existence of the first two is well-attested and their ascription to Ibn Ezra is certain, the very existence of the third work and the association with him of the fourth necessitate an explanation. We turn now to survey these works and discuss their links with Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot. Ibn Ezra wrote the two redactions of Sefer ha-Te‘amim to flesh out the raw concepts formulated in Reshit Hokhmah. Te‘amim I lacks an introduction from which readers could learn its purpose, but it is full of quotations from Reshit Hokhmah; it is organized in the same 10 chapters and the incipits of its glosses match the order in which those words or phrases appear in Reshit Hokhmah. By contrast, in the opening sentence of Teamim II Ibn Ezra promises “to lay the foundation of the Book of the Beginning of Wisdom.” Nevertheless, even though Teamim II includes obvious quotations from an underlying text and many parts of Jeamim II do not make sense unless one assumes that they are commenting on quoted passages, it is virtually impossible to find explicit and obvious quotations from Reshit Hokhmah in Te‘amim II. Moreover, some parts of Teamim II address concepts related to nativities, which are never touched on in Reshit Hokhmah. In addition, in the many cases where Reshit Hokhmah and Te‘amim Il discuss similar topics, they do so in a different order. What text is Te‘amim II commenting on, then? In four manuscripts, dat- ing from the sixteenth, eighteenth, and nineteenth centuries, the copyists established a clear link between Jeamim II and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot by arranging sections of these texts in parallel columns. The theory that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot was the text on which Teamim II expands was later taken up by Moritz Steinschneider and particularly JL. Fleischer. A closer look, however, renders this hypothesis untenable: if Teamim Il comments on quotations and ideas from Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, the latter should con- tain the vast majority of the quotations, employ the same terminology, and follow the same order of presentation as Te‘amim II. But a comparison of parallel sections in TJeamim II and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot demonstrates that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot meets none of these three requisites. Given that at the very start of Jeamim II Ibn Ezra wrote that “I wish to lay the foundation of the Book of Reshit Hokhmah,’ we are forced to conclude that Te‘amim II is commenting ona second redaction of Reshit Hokhmah (hence- forth Reshit Hokhmah II). My examination of the entries under “Reshit Hokhmah (by Abraham Ibn Ezra)” in the catalogue of the Institute for Microfilmed Hebrew Manu- scripts, National Library of Israel, Jerusalem, and in other libraries, found that all of them are copies of Reshit Hokhmah, the text presented in this volume. I then tried to identify the text of Reshit Hokhmah Il, or at least a fragment of it, in manuscripts catalogued under other names, and par- ticularly in anthologies of Hebrew astrological texts that circulated in the Middle Ages and were ascribed to Ibn Ezra. This approach paid off. Hidden in a larger work ascribed to Abraham Ibn Ezra, entitled Sefer ha-Sheelot (Book of Interrogations), I found a fragment that, as I showed in a sepa- rate study, was part of Reshit Hokhmah II. The main part of the fragment consists of the presentation of 34 categories of signs. In a nutshell, my argu- ment is based on the fact that an overwhelming majority of these categories are virtually identical with those commented on in Jeamim II, and that in some cases these categories appear only in the fragment and in Teamim IL. Links between Reshit Hokhmah Il, on the one hand, and Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, on the other, will be shown in the notes to these texts. The Epitome totius astrologiae, dated to 1142, and attributed to John of Seville, consists of an introductory book, the Ysagoge in Astrologiam, and the Liber quadripartitus, which addresses the four main subdivisions of Arabic astrology: world astrology, nativities, interrogations, and elections. Charles Burnett first advanced this thesis in Burnett 2002, 75-77. Later he displayed the close links between the Liber primus, de gentibus, regibus, civitatibus, aeris mutatione, fame et mortalitate, the second component of the Liber quadripartitus, and the Tractatus pluviarum et aeris mutationis, most of which comes from Liber primus de gentibus. See Burnett 2008. Recently Burnett has referred to the links between the Epitome totius astrologiae and Reshit Hokhmah. Smithuis, 2004, especially chapter 3. See Sela 2010,
translation: Beyond Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, Ibn Ezra composed or was involved to some extent in the creation of several treatises belonging to the genre of astrological introductions. These include the two versions of Sefer ha-Te'amim, the second version of Reshit Hokhmah, and Ysagoge, which forms the first part of the Epitome totius astrologiae, a popular twelfth-century Latin work considered to be within Ibn Ezra's astrological corpus. While the existence of the first two is well-attested and their attribution to Ibn Ezra is certain, the very existence of the third work and its association with him require explanation. We will now survey these works and discuss their connections with Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot. Ibn Ezra wrote the two redactions of Sefer ha-Te'amim to elaborate on the fundamental concepts presented in Reshit Hokhmah. Te'amim I lacks an introduction explaining its purpose but is rich in quotations from Reshit Hokhmah; it is structured into the same 10 chapters, and the beginnings of its glosses correspond to the order in which those words or phrases appear in Reshit Hokhmah. In contrast, in the opening sentence of Te'amim II, Ibn Ezra promises "to lay the foundation of the Book of the Beginning of Wisdom." Nevertheless, even though Te'amim II contains clear quotations from an underlying text, and many parts of Te'amim II are incomprehensible unless one assumes they are commenting on quoted passages, it is virtually impossible to find explicit and obvious quotations from Reshit Hokhmah in Te'amim II. Furthermore, some parts of Te'amim II address concepts related to nativities, which are never discussed in Reshit Hokhmah. Additionally, in many instances where Reshit Hokhmah and Te'amim II discuss similar topics, they do so in a different order. So, what text is Te'amim II commenting on? In four manuscripts, dating from the sixteenth, eighteenth, and nineteenth centuries, copyists established a clear link between Te'amim II and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot by arranging sections of these texts in parallel columns. The theory that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot was the text on which Te'amim II expands was later adopted by Moritz Steinschneider and particularly J.L. Fleischer. A closer examination, however, renders this hypothesis untenable: if Te'amim II comments on quotations and ideas from Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, the latter should contain the vast majority of the quotations, employ the same terminology, and follow the same order of presentation as Te'amim II. But a comparison of parallel sections in Te'amim II and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot demonstrates that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot meets none of these three requirements. Given that at the very beginning of Te'amim II Ibn Ezra wrote that "I wish to lay the foundation of the Book of Reshit Hokhmah," we are forced to conclude that Te'amim II is commenting on a second redaction of Reshit Hokhmah (henceforth Reshit Hokhmah II). My examination of the entries under "Reshit Hokhmah (by Abraham Ibn Ezra)" in the catalog of the Institute for Microfilmed Hebrew Manuscripts, National Library of Israel, Jerusalem, and in other libraries, found that all of them are copies of Reshit Hokhmah, the text presented in this volume. I then attempted to identify the text of Reshit Hokhmah II, or at least a fragment of it, in manuscripts cataloged under other names, and particularly in anthologies of Hebrew astrological texts that circulated in the Middle Ages and were attributed to Ibn Ezra. This approach proved fruitful. Hidden in a larger work attributed to Abraham Ibn Ezra, titled Sefer ha-She'elot (Book of Interrogations), I found a fragment that, as I showed in a separate study, was part of Reshit Hokhmah II. The main part of the fragment consists of the presentation of 34 categories of signs. In short, my argument is based on the fact that an overwhelming majority of these categories are virtually identical with those commented on in Te'amim II, and that in some cases these categories appear only in the fragment and in Te'amim II. Links between Reshit Hokhmah II, on the one hand, and Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, on the other, will be shown in the notes to these texts. The Epitome totius astrologiae, dated to 1142 and attributed to John of Seville, consists of an introductory book, the Ysagoge in Astrologiam, and the Liber quadripartitus, which addresses the four main subdivisions of Arabic astrology: world astrology, nativities, interrogations, and elections. Charles Burnett first put forward this thesis in Burnett 2002, 75-77. Later, he demonstrated the close links between the Liber primus, de gentibus, regibus, civitatibus, aeris mutatione, fame et mortalitate, the second component of the Liber quadripartitus, and the Tractatus pluviarum et aeris mutationis, most of which comes from Liber primus de gentibus. See Burnett 2008. Recently, Burnett has referred to the links between the Epitome totius astrologiae and Reshit Hokhmah. Smithuis, 2004, especially chapter 3. See Sela 2010.
chunk_ref: aatsbibliothek 220 [Or. Qu. 679] (IMHM: F 1779), fol. 31b; Paris, Biblio- théque

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah's Wide Diffusion
text: מכל ספרי אבן עזרא באסטרולוגיה, רשית חכמה היתה הנפוצה ביותר בקרב יהודים בימי הביניים ואחריהם, כפי שמעיד העובדה שהיא שרדה בלמעלה מ-70 עותקי כתבי יד (המספר הגדול ביותר לכל אחד מחיבוריו האסטרולוגיים).
translation: Of all Ibn Ezra's astrological works, Reshit Hokhmah had the widest diffusion among Jews in the Middle Ages and afterward, as attested by the fact that it survives in no fewer than 70 manuscript copies (the largest number for any of his astrological treatises).
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah in Southern France
text: בתוך שלושה עשורים ממות מחברו, רשית חכמה היתה נפוצה ברחבי אגן הים התיכון, מפרובנס ועד מצרים. בשנים האחרונות של המאה השתים עשרה, חוג של חוקרים בדרום צרפת הפנה סדרה של שאלות לרמב"ם במצרים, בבקשה לעצתו בנוגע לכמה נושאים מטרידים הקשורים לאסטרולוגיה.
translation: Within three decades of its author's death, Reshit Hokhmah was circulating throughout the Mediterranean basin, from Provence to Egypt. In the closing years of the twelfth century, a circle of scholars in southern France addressed a series of queries to Maimonides in Egypt, requesting his advice on some troubling issues related to astrology.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah in Northern France
text: זמן קצר לאחר מות אבן עזרא, רשית חכמה הגיעה לצפון צרפת, שם יהודים היו ידועים בהעדפתם ללימודי תלמוד על פני מדע ופילוסופיה. ספר החיים האנונימי, שנכתב בסביבות 1200 בצפון צרפת, משתמש בטרמינולוגיה של רשית חכמה מספר פעמים, וקטע שלם מבוסס על מידע שנלקח ממנה.
translation: Shortly after Ibn Ezra's death, Reshit Hokhmah reached northern France, where Jews were known for their preference for Talmudic studies over science and philosophy. The anonymous Sefer ha-Hayyim, written around 1200 in northern France, employs the terminology of Reshit Hokhmah several times, and an entire passage is based on information drawn from it.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: The Term 'Toledet' in Mahzor Vitry
text: בנוסף, החלק האסטרולוגי של כתב היד המוקדם ביותר של מחזור ויטרי, שנכתב בצפון צרפת במאה השתים עשרה, משתמש במונח הייחודי 'תולדת', מונח חדשני שטבע אבן עזרא והשתמש בו לאורך כל יצירתו כדי לציין את מושג הטבע בהקשר של סימני גלגל המזלות וארבעת היסודות.
translation: Additionally, the astrological section of the earliest manuscript of the Mahzor Vitry, composed in northern France in the twelfth century, employs the peculiar term 'toledet', a neologism coined by Ibn Ezra and used throughout his oeuvre to denote the concept of nature in the context of the zodiacal signs and the four elements.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah in Encyclopedias
text: החל מהמאה השלוש עשרה, רשית חכמה הועברה גם באמצעות קטעים באנציקלופדיות ובאנתולוגיות אסטרולוגיות, סימן לפופולריות הגוברת שלה. המוקדמת שבהן היא ספר הכולל, אנציקלופדיה אסטרולוגית ואסטרונומית עצומה שהורכבה בשנת 1256 וכללה במקור לפחות 40 חלקים, מתוכם רק תשעת האחרונים קיימים, בשני חלקים נפרדים.
translation: Starting in the thirteenth century, Reshit Hokhmah was also transmitted by means of excerpts in astrological encyclopedias and anthologies, a sign of its increasing popularity. The earliest of these is Sefer ha-Kolel (the Comprehensive Book), a vast astrological and astronomical encyclopedia composed in 1256 and originally consisting of at least 40 sections, of which only the last nine are extant, in two separate portions.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah in Liwyat Hen
text: מקרה נוסף הוא פרק 40, על אסטרולוגיה, של לווית חן, האנציקלופדיה המדעית שהורכבה על ידי לוי בן אברהם בן חיים (1235-1305) בפרובנס לאחר 1276. לא בוצע עד כה מיפוי שיטתי של עבודה זו, אך אפילו בדיקה שטחית מגלה ציטוטים נרחבים מפרק 9 של רשית חכמה ואזכור מפורש של עבודה זו כמקורם.
translation: Another case is chapter 40, on astrology, of Liwyat hen (Chaplet of Grace), the scientific encyclopedia composed by Levi ben Abraham ben Hayyim (1235-1305) in Provence after 1276. No systematic mapping of this work has been so far carried out, but even a superficial scrutiny discovers extensive quotations from chapter 9 of Reshit Hokhmah and an explicit mention of that work as their source.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Reshit Hokhmah in Sefer ha-She'elot
text: דוגמה נוספת היא ספר השאלות, שנכתב לא יאוחר מהשנים הראשונות של המאה החמש עשרה. כותרתו טוענת שאבן עזרא הוא המחבר, אך עבודה זו, למרות שמה, אינה זהה לאף אחת משלוש העריכות של ספרו שאלות. למעשה, זוהי אנתולוגיה של קטעים אסטרולוגיים באורכים ומקורות שונים, שנלקחו בעיקר מחיבוריו האסטרולוגיים של אבן עזרא.
translation: A further example is Sefer ha-She'elot (the Book of Interrogations), written no later than the first years of the fifteenth century. Its header claims Ibn Ezra as the author, but this work, despite its title, is not identical with any of the three redactions of his Sefer ha-She'lot. In fact, it is an anthology of astrological fragments of diverse lengths and origins, taken mainly from Ibn Ezra's astrological treatises.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Commentaries on Reshit Hokhmah
text: העניין ברשית חכמה והשפעתה על המחשבה היהודית המאוחרת יותר ניכר גם במספר הרב של פירושים עליה. אחד מהם הוא הפירוש על אוסף חיבורים אסטרולוגיים של אבן עזרא שהורכב על ידי 'מאסטרה ליאון', שהיה כנראה הפילוסוף והמדען הגדול של המאה הארבע עשרה, גרשונידס (1288-1344).
translation: The interest in Reshit Hokhmah and its impact on subsequent Jewish thought may also be gauged by the large number of commentaries on it. One of them is the commentary on a collection of Ibn Ezra's astrological treatises composed by 'Maestre Leon,' who was probably the great fourteenth-century philosopher and scientist Gersonides (1288-1344).
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot's Diffusion
text: משפטי המזלות קיימת בלפחות 25 עותקי כתבי יד, הרבה פחות מרשית חכמה, שפופולריותה הגדולה האפילה עליה. אולם, כאשר אנו משווים זאת לחיבוריו האסטרולוגיים האחרים של אבן עזרא, כגון מולדות (53 עותקים קיימים), מאורות (35 עותקים), עולם א' (34 עותקים), שאלות א' (29 עותקים), מבחרים ב' (28 עותקים), עולם ב' (26 עותקים), שאלות ב' (18 עותקים), ותקופה (4 עותקים), אנו יכולים להסיק שמשפטי המזלות נהנתה מתפוצה רחבה יחסית.
translation: Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot is extant in at least 25 manuscript copies, many fewer than Reshit Hokhmah, whose great popularity eclipsed it. However, when we compare this to Ibn Ezra's other astrological treatises, such as Moladot (53 extant copies), Meorot (35 copies), 'Olam I (34 copies), Sheelot I (29 copies), Mivharim II (28 copies), 'Olam II (26 copies), Sheelot II (18 copies), and Tequfah (4 copies), we may infer that Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot enjoyed a relatively wide diffusion.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot in Gersonides' Library
text: שמשפטי המזלות נחשבה בערכה רב יותר מ-150 שנה לאחר מות אבן עזרא ונראתה כמייצגת את כלל יצירתו האסטרולוגית ניכרת בכותרות של שלושת הפריטים בקטלוג הספרייה הפרטית של גרשונידס שהם אסטרולוגיים בבירור. כולם כוללים את הביטוי משפטי המזלות; באחד מהם זוהי התייחסות ישירה למשפטי המזלות, המראה שהאחרון היה בספרייתו הפרטית של גרשונידס.
translation: That Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot was held in high esteem more than 150 years after Ibn Ezra's death and was seen as representative of his entire astrological corpus is evident in the titles of the three items in the catalogue of Gersonides' private library that are clearly astrological. All include the phrase mishpetei ha-mazzalot; in one of them this is a direct reference to Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, showing that the latter was in Gersonides' private library.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Ibn Ezra's Terminology in Gersonides
text: זה מאושר על ידי העובדה שגרשונידס, באחד מפירושיו המקראיים, משתמש בביטוי אסטרולוגי שטבע אבן עזרא והשתמש בו רק במשפטי המזלות.
translation: This is confirmed by the fact that Gersonides, in one of his biblical commentaries, employs an astrological expression coined by Ibn Ezra and used by him only in Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Translations for Christian Readers
text: החל מהמחצית השנייה של המאה השלוש עשרה, רשית חכמה ומשפטי המזלות הונגשו לקוראים נוצרים באמצעות תרגומים לצרפתית עתיקה, לטינית, ספרדית עתיקה, קטלאנית ושפות אירופאיות אחרות. בסביבות אותה תקופה, הנרי באט ממלינס (1246-1310 לערך), סטודנט לתאולוגיה ואמנויות באוניברסיטת פריז שהיה בקיא באסטרונומיה ואסטרולוגיה, הזמין את חגין היהודי לתרגם אוסף של יצירות אסטרולוגיות של אבן עזרא מעברית לצרפתית כדי שיהיו זמינות.
translation: Starting in the second half of the thirteenth century, Reshit Hokhmah and Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot were made available to Christian readers via translations into Old French, Latin, Old Spanish, Catalan, and other European languages. Around that time, Henry Bate of Malines (1246-ca. 1310), a student in theology and the arts at the University of Paris who was proficient in astronomy and astrology, commissioned Hagin le Juif to translate a collection of Ibn Ezra's astrological works from Hebrew into French so that they would be available.
chunk_ref: 27-28, and notes to the English translations of ‘Olam I and ‘Olam II. 191 See S

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Manuscript O: Oxford Bodleian Library
text: ע Oxford, Bodleian Library, Add. Qu. 160 [Neubauer 2518] (IMHM: F 22230), fols. 14la—154a. This is an octavo paper manuscript of 179 folios, written in a Greek rabbinical script in Thebes in 1367. The manuscript is one of the earliest, if not the earliest, with a collection of astrological works by Ibn Ezra: Reshit Hokhmah, fols. 13a—65b; Teamim I, fols. 65b—87b; Moladot, fols. 88a—117b; Me’orot, fols. 118a—126a; Mivharim I, fols. 128b—140a; ‘Olam I, fols. 141a—154a; ‘Olam Il, fols. 165a—167b (written in a seventeenth-century hand). Used as copy text for the critical edition of Reshit Hokhmah.
translation: O: Oxford, Bodleian Library, Add. Qu. 160 [Neubauer 2518] (IMHM: F 22230), fols. 141a–154a. This is an octavo paper manuscript of 179 folios, written in a Greek rabbinical script in Thebes in 1367. The manuscript is one of the earliest, if not the earliest, with a collection of astrological works by Ibn Ezra: Reshit Hokhmah, fols. 13a–65b; Teamim I, fols. 65b–87b; Moladot, fols. 88a–117b; Me'orot, fols. 118a–126a; Mivharim I, fols. 128b–140a; 'Olam I, fols. 141a–154a; 'Olam II, fols. 165a–167b (written in a seventeenth-century hand). Used as copy text for the critical edition of Reshit Hokhmah.
chunk_ref: See note there. This term is used in Gersonides’ commentary on 1 Kings 18:27, w

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Manuscript S: Philadelphia Schoenberg Collection
text: ש Philadelphia, University of Pennsylvania, Schoenberg Collection, Ljs 57/2, formerly MS Sassoon 823, (IMHM: F 09357). Vellum, 228 pp. 278 x 210 mm, eight-leaf quires, written in an early Spanish hand, very likely of the four- teenth century. Contains: Tables of Jacob ben David Po‘el, pp. 1-25; Reshit Hokhmah, pp. 25-60; Mivharim II (fragment from the introduction), pp. 61— 62; She’elot II (beginning is lacking, fragment from the introduction until end of eighth place), pp. 63-68; Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot, pp. 69-86; ‘Olam I, pp. 86-93; Sefer ha-Mivharim li-Batlamyus (Book of Elections by Ptolemy), pp. 93-94; astronomical and calendrical tables, pp. 95-228. Used as copy text for the critical edition of Mishpetei ha-Mazzalot and as one of the wit- nesses for the critical edition of Reshit Hokhmah.

translation: In the name of God, who gives strength to the weary and increases power to the powerless, I begin to write the Book of the Beginning of Wisdom. 1 (1) The beginning of wisdom is the fear of the Lord, for it is the foundation. For when a person does not seek after his desires, and his heart is not filled with them, then wisdom will rest within him. (2) Moreover, the fear of the Lord will guard him from the statutes and governance all the days of his life, and when his soul separates from his body, it will bequeath him existence and life forever. (3) And behold, I will begin to recount the laws of the heavens in the way of judgments, as the ancients tested, generation after generation. (4) And after I complete this book, I will compose a book explaining the reasons. (5) And to God I will turn for help. 2 (1) This book is divided into ten gates. (2) The first gate concerns the form of the sphere and its parts, its signs and figures, and the seven planets, their degrees, their movement, and their dominion. (3) The second gate concerns the nature of the signs, their degrees and their actions, and the mixture of the stars and figures. (4) The third gate concerns the nature of the degrees, their strength and their changes, their love and their hatred, and the parts of the quadrants of the sphere, and the twelve houses. (5) The fourth gate concerns the mixture of the seven servants and their strength, and all that they indicate in all created beings that are upon the earth. (6) The fifth gate concerns the words of the servants, when their strength increases and when it diminishes. (7) The sixth gate concerns the strength of the servants themselves, and according to their being before the Sun and after it. (8) The seventh gate concerns the aspects of the servants, and their conjunctions and their mixtures and their differences, and their words that their strength will mix one with another, concerning all that they indicate. (9) The eighth gate concerns the judgments of the servants in questions, and in nativities, and in revolutions. (10) The ninth gate concerns the lots of the servants, and the lots of the houses, and all other lots that the wise men of the signs mentioned. (11) The tenth gate concerns the rising of the light of the seven, their custom and their transfer upon the degrees of the sphere, and all their matters.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: The Heavens and Earth's Movements
text: שיחקור על זאת החכמה יתבונן בתנועת השבעה המשרתים, שהם מהירים בהליכתם מתנועתם סביבות מעלות הגלגל העליון, ותנועתם בגלגליהם ובהיותם עם הכוכבים העליונים שהם בגלגל המזלות, וכל אלה התנועות שהם סביב המוצק, שהוא הארץ, שהוא כמו נקודה בתוך העגולה. (2) אז יתברר בליבו כי התנועות הנזכרות, אעפ שתנועתם שוות ישרות, יתחלף מעשיהם כפי הגבולים. (3) וזה הדבר בגלגל במספר מעלות הגלגל. (4) וצורותיו השמאליות והדרומיות, ודעת השבעה המשרתים ותולדותם, והכללים והפרטים, וכל מעשיהם.
translation: Whoever investigates this wisdom will understand the movement of the seven servants, who are swift in their motion around the degrees of the upper sphere, and their movement in their own spheres, and their being with the upper stars that are in the sphere of the constellations. All these movements are around the solid, which is the Earth, which is like a point within the circle. (2) Then it will become clear in his heart that the aforementioned movements, even though their motion is equally direct, their actions will differ according to the boundaries. (3) And this matter is in the sphere, in the number of degrees of the sphere. (4) And its northern and southern forms, and the knowledge of the seven servants and their effects, and the general and specific rules, and all their actions.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra
work: Introductions to Astrology (Beginning of Wisdom & Judgments of the Zodiacal Signs)
title: Chapter 1: Divisions of the Zodiac
text: השער הראשון. (1) הגלגל נחלק לשלש מאות וששים חלקים שוים נקראו מעלות. (2) ועל זה הסכימה דעת כל הקדמונים והאחרונים, בי יש למספר הזה השברים שיש מחצי עד עשירית מהשביעית. (3) גם הוא נחלק לשנים עשר חלקים, יקראו מזלות, וכל מזל שלשים מעלות ישרות, וכל מעלה ששים ראשונים, ובל ראשון ששים שניים, ובבה עד עשיריים.
translation: Chapter One. (1) The sphere is divided into three hundred and sixty equal parts called degrees. (2) On this, the opinion of all the ancients and moderns agreed, that this number has fractions from a half to a tenth of a seventh. (3) It is also divided into twelve parts, called signs, and each sign has thirty straight degrees, and each degree has sixty firsts, and each first has sixty seconds, and so on up to tenths.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra

title: Names of the Zodiac Signs
text: ושמות המזלות: טלה, שור, תאומים, סרטן, אריה, בתולה, מאזנים, עקרב, קשת, גדי, דלי, דגים. (2) ואלה הם בגלגל העליון, שהוא השמיני, עם הצורות השמאליות והדרומיות. (3) והנה בו מ
translation: And the names of the signs are: Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces. (2) And these are in the upper sphere, which is the eighth, with the northern and southern forms. (3) And behold, in it are forty-eight forms.

title: Aries Decans and Interpretations
text: (21) ויעלה בפנים הראשונים ממנו צורת אשה היא המאירה, וזנב דג מן הים בדמות אפעה, וראש המשולש, וצורת שור. (22) ויאמרו אנשי הודו בי בו ראש צורת כלב, בידו השמאלית נר ובידו הימנית מפתח. (23) ויאמר כנבה, הוא חכם שלהם, בי יעלה בפנים הראשונים איש שחורות וגבותיו ישרות, והוא מן הנפילים, והוא מתהלל בנפשו, והוא מתעטף באדרת לבנה גדולה, ועליו אזור חבל, והוא בעסן, עומד על רגליו. (24) ועל דעת בטלמיוס יעלה בו גב האשה היושבת על הכסא, וארכובותיה וידה השמאלית, וחצי גב האשה שאין לה בעל עם פחדיה ושוליה, והדג השני וקצת חוטי הפשתים. (25) ויעלה עם הפנים השניים דגים, ואמצעית המשולש, וחצי חיה, ואשה יש מסרק בראשה, ושריון נחושת, וראש השטן. (26) ויאמרו אנשי הודו בי יעלה בו צורת אשה מתעטפת בבגדים ואדרת, ויש לה רגל אחד וצורתה צורת סוסיה. (27) ועל דעת בטלמיוס תעלה היושבת על הכסא, וראש הנושא את השטן וכפו הימנית, ושולי האשה בלא בעל, ורגליה, והמשולש, וראש טלה וקרניו, והנשאר מחוטי הפשתים. (28) ויעלה בפנים השלישיים בחור יושב על כסא עם מכסה ובידו תרפים, ואדם השפיל ראשו למטה והוא צועק אל השם; גם יעלה בו גחון של דג וראשו, וסוף המשולש והחצי השני מן החיה. (29) ויאמרו חכמי הודו בי יעלה בו איש אדום ושערו אדמדם, והוא בעסן הולך בקרי, ובידו צמידים מעצים ושרביט, ובגדיו אדומים, והוא נושא ברזל, ורצונו לעשות טוב ולא יוכל. (30) ויעלה על דעת בטלמיוס הנושא את ראש השטן וגוף טלה. (31) והנולד בו יהיה גופו ממוסך, ופניו ארוכים ועיניו גדולים, והוא מביט אל הארץ הרבה, וצוארו עב ואזניו רפות, ויש לו שער רב קווצותיו תלתלים, שוקיו רכות, ומדברו נאה וידבר צדק מדי, מתאוה לאכול הרבה, והוא אוהב צדק.
translation: (21) In its first decan, the figure of a woman, the shining one, rises, and the tail of a sea fish in the form of a viper, and the head of the triangle, and the figure of a bull. (22) The people of India say that in it rises the head of the figure of a dog, with a lamp in its left hand and a key in its right hand. (23) Kanakah, their sage, said that in the first decan rises a dark-skinned man with straight eyebrows, and he is one of the giants, and he praises himself, and he is wrapped in a large white cloak, and he has a rope belt, and he is angry, standing on his feet. (24) And according to Ptolemy, in it rises the back of the Woman Sitting on the Throne, and her knees and her left hand, and half the back of the Woman Who Has No Husband with her thighs and her hem, and the second Fish and some of the flaxen threads. (25) And with the second decan rise fish, and the middle of the triangle, and half a beast, and a woman with a comb on her head, and a copper armor, and the head of the devil. (26) And the people of India say that in it rises the figure of a woman wrapped in clothes and a cloak, and she has one leg and her form is like that of a mare. (27) And according to Ptolemy, the Woman Sitting on the Throne rises, and the head of the Bearer of the Devil and his right hand, and the hem of the Woman Who Has No Husband, and her legs, and the Triangle, and the head of Aries and its horns, and the remainder of the flaxen threads. (28) And in the third decan rises a young man sitting on a throne with a cover and teraphim in his hand, and a man who has lowered his head and is crying out to God; also in it rises the belly of a fish and its head, and the end of the triangle and the other half of the beast. (29) And the sages of India say that in it rises a red man with reddish hair, and he is angry, walking crookedly, and in his hand are wooden bracelets and a scepter, and his clothes are red, and he carries iron, and he wishes to do good but cannot. (30) And according to Ptolemy, the Bearer of the Devil's head and the body of Aries rise. (31) And one born under it will have a mixed body, and a long face and large eyes, and he looks much at the ground, and his neck is thick and his ears are soft, and he has much hair with curly locks, his shins are soft, and his speech is pleasant and he speaks justice often, desiring to eat much, and he loves justice.

(29) The Indian scientists say that a yellow man with reddish hair rises there; he is irascible and defiant. On his arms are wooden bracelets and (he carries) a scepter, his clothes are red. He is a blacksmith and wishes to do good but cannot. His neck will be thick and his ears flaccid. He will have abundant curly hair, his shanks will be thin, his speech will be agreeable and he will be garrulous. (37) ותחלואיו ina‏ שנופל פתאום ולא ירגיש, וכאב האזנים והנחירים והשן והעינים, והבהרות שהן? כדמות פצעים. translation: In its third decan, a young man sitting in a chair, covered, with talismans in his hand, rises, along with a man whose head is bowed, praying to the Lord. The fish's belly and head also rise there, and the end of the triangle, and the second half of the snake. The Indian scientists say that a yellow man with reddish hair rises there; he is irascible and defiant. On his arms are wooden bracelets and he carries a scepter, his clothes are red. He is a blacksmith and wishes to do good but cannot. According to Ptolemy, the Carrier of the Devil's Head [Perseus] and the body of (the constellation) Aries rise (in it). A person born in it [who has Aries as the ascendant of his nativity] will have a mixed body, a long face and large eyes; he will tend to look towards the ground. His neck will be thick and his ears flaccid. He will have abundant curly hair, his shanks will be thin, his speech will be agreeable and he will be garrulous. He will be a glutton, irascible, a lover of justice, and his voice will not be strong. If the Moon was in this sign, or its lot, or one of the malefic planets, it indicates a bad smell, leprosy, deafness, baldness, and a receding hairline. One born in the first decan will be yellow, with a narrow belly and little flesh, and will have a mark on his left leg and likewise on his left arm. He will have many friends and hate evil. One born in the second decan will have darkness with a beautiful face, and his body will be mixed, quick to anger but not holding grudges, high-minded, with complete wisdom and morals, and many enemies. One born in the third decan will be yellow, and astonished by people. This sign rules the head, the face, the pupil of the eye, and the ears. Its ailments are sudden falling without feeling, and pain in the ears, nostrils, teeth, and eyes, and spots that resemble wounds. According to the Egyptian scientists, the pain of Saturn in it is the chest, Jupiter the heart, Mars the head, the Sun the fears, Venus the feet, Mercury the shins, and the Moon the knees. This sign rules among people, kings who are righteous kings, and the generous, and those involved in wars, fire, killing, and blood, and those who travel on roads. It is the house of Mars, and the exaltation of the Sun is at 19 degrees, and the detriment of Saturn is at 21 degrees, and the hatred of Venus, and the place of Mercury's fall is at 20 degrees at this time, which is the year 4908. The lords of the triplicity are the Sun, and after it Jupiter, and by night Jupiter, and after it the Sun, and their partner by day and by night is Saturn. The first decan, according to the Egyptian scientists and most of the foreign scientists, is for Mars, the second for the Sun, and the third for Venus; and according to the Indian scientists, the first decan is for Mars, the second for the Sun, and the third for Jupiter. These are the bounds according to the Egyptian and Babylonian scientists: for Jupiter six degrees, for Venus six, for Mercury eight, for Mars five, and for Saturn five; and according to Ptolemy, for Jupiter six at the beginning, and the second for Venus eight, and the third for Mercury seven, and the fourth for Mars five, and the fifth for Saturn four. The beginning of the novenaries from it is for Mars, and the second for Venus, the third for Mercury, the fourth for the Moon, the fifth for the Sun, the sixth for Mercury, the seventh for Venus, the eighth for Mars, and the ninth for Jupiter; and behold, each novenary is three and one-third degrees, and all the novenaries are according to the nature of the sign and the lord of the sign. And the strength of the twelve: the first for Mars, the second for Venus, the third for Mercury, the fourth for the Moon, the fifth for the Sun, the sixth for Mercury. Its voice is not strong! translation: to the Sun, and the third to Saturn. (38) These are the terms according to the Egyptian sages and all astrologers: for Venus up to eight degrees, then for Mercury six, then for Jupiter eight, then for Saturn five, then for Mars three; and according to Ptolemy: for Venus eight, for Mercury seven, for Jupiter seven, for Mars two, and for Saturn six. (39) The first and second parts of the ninth-part are for Saturn, the third for Jupiter, the fourth for Mars, the fifth for Venus, the sixth for Mercury, the seventh for the Moon, the eighth for the Sun, and the ninth for Mercury. (40) The beginning of the dodecatemoria is for Venus, the second for Mercury, the third for the Moon, the fourth for the Sun, the fifth for Mercury, the sixth for Venus, the seventh for Mars, the eighth for Jupiter, the ninth and tenth for Saturn, the eleventh for Jupiter, and the twelfth for Mars. (41) From the beginning of the sign to the end of three degrees, they are mixed; then two bright degrees, then two empty degrees, then eight bright degrees, then five empty degrees, then three bright degrees, and after them two mixed degrees. (42) From the beginning of the sign up to seven degrees, they are masculine; after them eight feminine degrees, and after that fifteen masculine degrees. (43) The pits of the stars: the fifth degree, and the twelfth, and the eighteenth, and the twenty-fourth, and the twenty-fifth, and the twenty-sixth. (44) The degrees that add grace and honor are the third, and the fifteenth, and the twenty-seventh, and the thirtieth. (45) In this sign, among the upper stars, there is the back of the leper, at the fourteenth degree, with a northern latitude of twenty-two degrees and twenty minutes, of the second magnitude, and of the nature of Venus. (46) And the head of the devil, which is at the fifteenth degree, with a northern latitude of twenty-three degrees and three minutes, of the second magnitude, and of the nature of Venus and Saturn. (47) And there is a bright star called the left eye of the bull, which is at the twenty-eighth degree, with a southern latitude of five and a half degrees, of the first magnitude, and of the nature of Mars and Venus; it is one of the stars that cause death when the lord of life's dominion reaches it. (48) And there is among the dim stars, a star at the tenth degree, with a northern latitude of twenty degrees and forty minutes. (49) And there is another dim star like it at the twelfth degree, with a northern latitude of forty and a half degrees, and it is one of the stars that cause death. According to the sages of India, the first decan is for Venus, the second for Mercury, and the third for Saturn.

translation: and its creeping things. (12) All of it indicates things with much movement, and every plant that is with water, and rainwater, and all sweet things. (13) It has the seventh of the climes, and the land of Lesser Armenia and that which is beyond Marqaf, and Azerbaijan, and Eastern Khurasan, and China, and parts of Africa, and Balkh. (14) In its portion are lakes, and every seashore, and every river bank, and trees of medium height. (15) From the letters, Dalet and Peh. (16) Its years are twenty-five, and so are its months, and the days are five, and so are the hours. (17) In the first decan rises the latter half of the Great Bear, and a complete figure wrapped in clothes, which is close to the singing figure; and there also rises an iron pig, its head of copper, and a virgin girl. (18) And the sages of India also said that there rises a handsome youth, clothed, with a golden pendant, and in his face and fingers is a slight deformity, and his body resembles the body of a horse and an elephant, and his legs are white, and on his body hang ornaments like trees, and he sits in a garden where aromatic cane grows. (19) And according to Ptolemy, in it rises the face of the Great Bear, and the head of the earlier and later Twins, and the hindquarters of the earlier Twin and his hands, and the Little Dog, and the remainder of the Great Dog, and the hull of the ship. (20) In the second decan rises a virgin girl, and she resembles a cloud; also rises there half of the dog, and half of the left ear of the donkey. (21) And the sages of India say that there rises a maiden of beautiful speech, and on her head is a myrtle crown, and in her hand a wooden staff, and she seeks wine and music. (22) And according to Ptolemy, in it rises the head of the Great Bear, and the later side of Cancer, and the belly of the ship.

text: ויעלה בפנים השלישיים נערה בתולה, והיא הולבת פעם למזרח פעם למערב; גם יעלה שם הכלב המאוחר, והחצי השני מאזני החמור השמאלי, גם החמור השני הדרומי.
translation: In the third decan, a virgin rises, sometimes going east and sometimes west; also rising there is the late dog, and the second half of the left donkey's scales, and also the second southern donkey.

text: הודו אומרים בי יעלה אדם ידמה רגלו לרגל חיה, ויש על גופו חיה, ובדעתו להכנס בספינה! ללכת בים להביא זהב וכסף, לעשות טבעות לנשיו.
translation: The Indians say that a man rises there whose leg resembles an animal's leg, and there is an animal on his body, and he intends to board a ship to go to sea to bring gold and silver, to make rings for his wives.

title: Natives Born in Cancer

translation: One born in it among humans will have thick limbs, a large forehead, separated teeth, and will be mute and deaf, loving people, and respected; but the nativity of women is not good, for it indicates difficult matters.

text: והנולד בפנים הראשונים יהיה נאה בגופו ובשערו, וגבות עיניו דבוקות?, ונחיריו ארוכים, וכתפיו רחבות, וסימן תחת אציל ידו או בזרועו הימנית, ונפשו טובה, ואוהביו רבים, והוא בעל מרמות.
translation: One born in the first decan will be handsome in body and hair, with joined eyebrows, long nostrils, and broad shoulders; he will have a mark under his armpit or on his right arm, and his soul will be good, and he will have many lovers, and he will be deceitful.

text: והוא בית הלבנה, וכבוד צדק במעלות חמש עשרה, וקלון מאדים במעלת שמנה ועשרים, ובית שנאת שבתאי | וראש התלי של צדק במעלה התשיעית, וראש התלי של שבתאי במעלת תשע עשרה.
translation: It is the house of the Moon, and Jupiter's exaltation is at fifteen degrees, and Mars's fall is at twenty-eight degrees, and it is the house of Saturn's detriment; and Jupiter's Head of the Dragon is at the ninth degree, and Saturn's Head of the Dragon is at the nineteenth degree.

translation: The lords of the triplicity by day are Venus and then Mars, and by night Mars and then Venus, and their partner by day and by night is the Moon.

text: הפנים הראשונים לדעת המצרים והבבלים לנגה, והשני כובב חמה, והשלישי לבנה; ועל דעת חכמי הודו הראשונים ללבנה, והשניים למאדים, והשלישיים לצדק.
translation: The first decan, according to the Egyptians and Babylonians, belongs to Venus, the second to Mercury, and the third to the Moon; and according to the Indian scientists, the first belongs to the Moon, the second to Mars, and the third to Jupiter.

text: ואלה הגבולים לדעת חכמי המצריים! וחכמי המזלות: למאדים שבע מעלות, ולנגה שש, ולכוכב חמה שש, ולצדק שבע, ולשבתאי ארבע; ועל דעת בטלמיוס למאדים שש, ולצדק שש, ולכוכב חמה ולנגה שבע, ולשבתאי ארבע2.
translation: These are the terms according to the Egyptian scientists and the astrologers: for Mars seven degrees, for Venus six, for Mercury six, for Jupiter seven, and for Saturn four; and according to Ptolemy: for Mars six, for Jupiter six, for Mercury and Venus seven, and for Saturn four.

translation: The first ninth-part belongs to the Moon, the second to the Sun, the third to Mercury, the fourth to Venus, the fifth to Mars, the sixth to Jupiter, the seventh and also the eighth to Saturn, and the ninth to Jupiter.

text: ותחלת כח שנים עשר ללבנה, השני לשמש, השלישי לכוכב החמה, הרביעי לנוגה, החמישי למאדים, הששי לצדק, והשביעי והשמיני לשבתאי, התשיעי לצדק+, העשירי למאדים, אחד עשר לנוגה, ושנים עשר לכוכב חמה.
translation: The first dodecatemoria belongs to the Moon, the second to the Sun, the third to Mercury, the fourth to Venus, the fifth to Mars, the sixth to Jupiter, the seventh and eighth to Saturn, the ninth to Jupiter, the tenth to Mars, the eleventh to Venus, and the twelfth to Mercury.

title: Virgo: Second Decan Imagery
text: (18) In its second decan, a shape clapping its hands and playing music rises. Half of the figure of a man rises there, too; his head is like the head of a bull, and there is half of a naked man in his hand; half of a beam with teeth at its end (Isa. 41:15), with which he plows the earth, rises there, too; the tail of the black snake and the middle of the lion rise there, too. (19) The Indian scientists say that an Ethiopian rises there; he is completely covered with hair and wears three garments, one of leather, the second of silk, the third a red cloak; he holds an inkwell!’ and is able to perform calculations. (20) In Ptolemy’s opinion, the end of the Dragon’s [Draco] tail rises there, and the tail of the Greater Bear [Ursa Major], the head and shoulder of (the constellation) Virgo, and the head, beak, and wings of the Crow [Corvus].
translation: (18) In its second decan, a figure clapping its hands and playing music ascends. Half of a man's figure also ascends there; his head is like the head of a bull, and there is half of a naked man in his hand; half of a beam with teeth at its end (Isaiah 41:15), with which he plows the earth, also ascends there; the tail of the black snake and the middle of the lion also ascend there. (19) The Indian scientists say that an Ethiopian ascends there; he is completely covered with hair and wears three garments, one of leather, the second of silk, the third a red cloak; he holds an inkwell and is able to perform calculations. (20) In Ptolemy’s opinion, the end of the Dragon’s tail ascends there, and the tail of the Greater Bear, the head and shoulder of Virgo, and the head, beak, and wings of Corvus.

author: Abraham Ibn Ezra

title: Virgo: Nativities and Body Parts
text: (24) A person born in it [who has Virgo as the ascendant of his nativity] will be of fine stature, with a straight and handsome body; he will know and understand; his hair will not be curly. He will love justice, his voice will be strong, he will be barren and benevolent, his face will be handsome, he will be a scribe and versed in mathematics. (25) One who is born in the second decan [a person who has the second decan of Virgo as the ascendant of his nativity] will be handsome; his eyes will be small and his nose beautiful; he will be moral, modest, generous, and love to be praised. (26) One who is born in the third decan, too, will be handsome, moral, and right-thinking, modest and wise. (27) Its portion of the human body is the stomach, the intestines, and the diaphragm. (28) The diseases affecting the aforementioned parts of the body are in its portion, and every disease whose cause is black bile.22
translation: (24) A person born in it will be of fine stature, with a straight and handsome body; he will know and understand; his hair will not be curly. He will love justice, his voice will be strong, he will be barren and benevolent, his face will be handsome, he will be a scribe and versed in mathematics. (25) One who is born in the second decan will be handsome; his eyes will be small and his nose beautiful; he will be moral, modest, generous, and love to be praised. (26) One who is born in the third decan, too, will be handsome, moral, and right-thinking, modest and wise. (27) Its portion of the human body is the stomach, the intestines, and the diaphragm. (28) The diseases affecting the aforementioned parts of the body are in its portion, and every disease whose cause is black bile.

translation: It trembles and shines; its beginning is changing and moist, its middle is mixed, and its end is noisy. When it is northern, it is moist, and when it is southern, it is cold. It is in the form of a scorpion. Its portion is the north-left, and a northern wind. The humor that dominates a person; and its taste, everything salty and bland; and the color of its appearance, red and green and the color of dust.

title: Scorpio First Decan: Rising Figures
text: ויעלה בפנים הראשונים אחורי סוס, ואחורי שור, ושחור בידו מקל. ואמרו חכמי הודו בי יעלה שם צורת אשה יפה, גופה אדום, והיא אובלת. ויעלה על דעת בטלמיוס יד הדוב הקטן, וראש הכלב וזרועו הימנית, ואמצעית המאזנים.
translation: In the first decan, the hindquarters of a horse rise, and the hindquarters of an ox, and a black man with a stick in his hand. The Indian scientists said that the figure of a beautiful woman rises there, her body red, and she is pregnant. According to Ptolemy, the hand of the Little Bear rises, and the head of the Dog and its right arm, and the middle of Libra.
